prevalence-in-rain-soil-water-bodies-and.text: Prevalence in rain, soil, water bodies, and air

remediation-of-per--and-polyfluoroalkyl-.text: Remediation of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances

pageProps.body:
Pfas

Notes on PFAS

PFAS

PFAS: Polyfluoroalkyl substances. Also called forever chemicals.

  • became widespread in 20th century
  • Used in non stick pans for example Concerns:
  • carcinogens that can cause health issues such as:
    • cancers
    • ulcerative colitis
    • thyroid disease
    • reduced immune response
  • Often found in
    • groundwater
    • waste water
    • soil

Definition

  • molecules that contain multiple fluorine atoms attached to an alkyl chain
    • alkyl: an alkane that is missing a single hydrogen atom
      • alkane: hydrogen and carbon atoms arranged in a tree structure where all carbon to carbon bonds are single.

Environmental Effects

Prevalence in rain, soil, water bodies, and air

  • Prevalence in rain water around the world is far more than the upper limit advisory for lifetime exposure.
  • Most common is TFA - Trifluoroacetic acid. It is ubiquitous
  • The PFAS are transferred from water to air, polluting the air and making it into rain. It is part of the water cycle.
  • Has reached places such as Antarctica.
  • High levels of contamination get into food supplies such as rice and coffee.
  • 31% of all groundwater surveyed in 2024 was polluted with PFAS

Bioaccumulation and biomagnification

Currently PFAS are accumulating in the food web. Surveying freshwater fish in the US in 2015, harmful levels were found.

Occupational exposure

Exposure from work leads to higher levels in blood

List of professions with high levels of blood PFAS:

  • Ski wax techs
  • Manufacturing workers: If the workers worked in a factory where PFAS are manufactured or used in manufacturing
  • Firefighters: because PFAS are found in Class B firefighting foams.

Mitigation measures

  • exposure monitoring
  • blood testing

Remediation

Water treatment

Broken down into destructive and non destructive remediation Non-Destructive:

  • Sorption
  • membrane filtering
  • foam fractionation
  • precipitation Destructive:
  • supercritical water oxidation
  • photodegradation
  • sonochemical odixation
  • electrochemical oxidation
  • plasma treatment
  • hydrothermal alkaline treatment

Remediation of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances

The destruction or removal of PFAS from the environment.

Destruction

As mentioned above. What happends to the fluorine though after desctruction? Is it safe once out of the PFAS?

Adsorption

Concentrated and removed from the environment. Then what?

concrete-steps-to-access-and-contribute-.text: Concrete steps to access and contribute to the DBGI Dendron

noteIndex.body:
![](/assets/images/allard-lab.jpg)

Welcome the the COMMONS Lab Open Dendron !

In the [COMMONS Lab](https://www.unifr.ch/bio/en/groups/allard/) we intent to follow the [Open Notebook Science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open-notebook_science) approach to document our research.

For this we use the [Dendron](https://www.dendron.so/) system as a mean to efficiently capture notes and publish them.

## What is this note

This note is a succinct tutorial note aiming to get you started in the use of the COMMONS Lab Dendron.

## Tutorial

### Dendron. 

#### What is Dendron ?

> Dendron is an open-source, local-first, markdown-based, note-taking tool. Think of it as a cache for everything that you care about - if you've spent more then five minutes solving a problem, you should never spent any more time solving the same exact problem.
> 
> Dendron is a knowledge base built by and for developers and integrates natively with IDEs like VS Code and VSCodium.

#### Where to get more info on Dendron ?

- You can get more information in the Dendron system at the official website www.dendron.so
- All the Dendron documentation is hosted here https://wiki.dendron.so/. It is, obviously, a Dendron itself.

#### Concrete steps to access and contribute to the DBGI Dendron

1. Install [VSCode](https://code.visualstudio.com/download)
2. Install [Dendron](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=dendron.dendron) from the VSCode marketplace 
3. Clone the COMMONS Dendron repo

    In your terminal
    ```
    git clone https://github.com/commons-research/commons-dws-public.git
    ```
4. In VSCode open (File/Open) the cloned repository.
5. Voila ! You should now be in the COMMONS Lab Open Dendron.

Feel free to explore and contribute.
A first, important step could be to configure VSCode so that you can easily add a daily note. See steps described at [[open-notebook.commons.setup]]


## Questions, comments, suggestions ?

Feel free to contribute here https://github.com/orgs/commons-research/discussions
